chapters.title: Lesson 01 Overview: Hugging Face Beyond Upload
First Break AI Cohort Setup and Lesson Page
Thought Experiment: What If Hugging Face Did Not Exist?
Hugging Face as the GitHub of AI Models
Why Hugging Face Exists Beyond GitHub
Exploring Qwen3 Files on Hugging Face
Do You Need Math for This Cohort?
Running Qwen3 in Pure C: Part 2 Preview
Two Worlds: Transformers vs Pure C
Loading Datasets with Python and Pandas
Finding Short and Long Dataset Examples

segments.text: Hey, everyone. Welcome to lesson number one. Welcome to First Break AI. If you are really looking to break into AI, this lesson is where things get really interesting.
Lesson number one is Hugging Face Beyond Upload. This is the lesson where we will break down the entire ecosystem for you.
We will run Qwen3, which is a small six hundred million parameter model. Locally, we will think of open models as supply chain.
And not only that, we will also look at h- an actual model. We'll try to visualize how a model looks like.
What is it required to run that model? What does it take to build a hub like Hugging Face? What to do if you have large datasets?
How to look at those large datasets? How to analyze them? We will look at GitHub. We will look at Markdown as a bonus.
We will look at how Markdown is used in almost all modern coding tools.
How is it— how it is supported in GitHub, how it is supported in Hugging Face and Discord.
We will also look at the three intuitions which drive this cohort, which is— which are very important right at the start.
We will also look at what are the model— what are the files when a model is uploaded to Hugging Face, what are the various files that are there, right?
So we have quite a lot to break down. This will be a longer video, and I hope that you get to enjoy, learn, and build along this journey, right?
So let's start our video with where we are at this point. So we are at First Break AI. First Break AI is a cohort which is open, and our goal in this cohort is to
is to build and learn at the same time. So there'll be a gui— this is a guided journey for you from commit to capstone, right?
This is a free and open cohort which allows you to upskill in training, inference, and AI product building.
Cohort has started If you have not joined or if you have joined just now, this cohort is open. You can drop in at any time and start looking at the material and start building, right?

It's a— The first cohort is for two months, from 1st May twenty twenty-six till 30 June twenty twenty-six. Even beyond this, these lessons are open, and you can choose when and how you want to cover this, right?
So we'll move to the first lesson. In order to reach the first lesson, you can either go to the Lessons link and view the lessons from there, or we have a ticker here.

Let's click on that. Actually, let me show you lessons. So this is the first lesson. And we have our introduction, and the second, this— lesson zero is our introduction, and we are at lesson one, which is Hugging Face Beyond Upload.
Right. So, yeah. So, as I mentioned, we— you must have covered lesson zero, Welcome to First Break AI, which basically deals with or enables you to build a blog
using any coding, AI coding tool. So you build a blog, preferably Quarto blog, using any AI IDE, be it Cursor, be it Claude Code or, Codex, whatever is your choice.
Right. Now let's look at the lesson itself. Before we begin, let's think of... Let me zoom this a bit, yeah.
Let's think of a thought experiment. Let, let's build a thought experiment. Imagine for a moment that something like Hugging Face doesn't exist, right?
By the way, Hugging Face is the, is the GitHub for AI models, right? So let's imagine something like this doesn't exist.
There is, there is no pip in, pip install transformers. There isn't any place where you can upload models, view datasets and all, right?
Imagine you are only given one file, which is the— one file or set of files, which are the actual model files. And you are asked to build something like ChatGPT, Claude Chat.
Basically, build an app where you ask a question and you get a reply. So you are expected to deploy that AI model.

What would it take to deploy that model, right? If Transformers does not exist, if Hugging Face does not exist, right? So to answer this question, first we will have to— What we'll do is we'll build Parallel worlds.
In one world where Hugging Face exists, what are the moving parts? What are the critical areas within Hugging Face? for example, we look at Z storage, we will look at Transformers library, we
look at datasets library, config.json, we look at tensor files, other things. And the second world, we will try and mirror this, you know.
So we'll try and run a model in Pure C and try and see, or try and map if Transformers does not exist, what do we have to do to run that file in our, in Pure C, right?
So let's look at the three intuitions first. What are the three intuitions? Right.
So, the first intuition is that modern language models, this is modern LLMs, are a stack of repeated blocks, right?
So you look at Qwen three, and you look at input text embedding, then decoder layer, RMSalom, linear, and then output, right?
And this can be expanded. So if I'm to go to this site, which is an... Which is a site which helps you visualize models interactively.
It's called HF Viewer, and it allows you to move the granularity. So, the granu-granularity at this moment is at the lowest level.
We move it up a bit, and we can see more details within the model. We move it up a bit, and we can see even more details, right? But at this point, we do not care about all these layers.
We care about input, we care about output, and something happens in between for this lesson. So this is what model looks like.
The first intuition is, whenever you imagine a model, think of this stack. Yes.
So think of this stack. That is the first. The second intuition is that LLMs generate one token at a time. So if you look at something like this, so you can see it is generating one token at a time.
The input text, the question that is asked here is, "Write a sonnet about love." So write a poem about love, and LLM starts writing a poem, but it's one token at a time, right?
So that's the second intuition. Third intuition is that the LLM, and this is a critical intuition, which is something that a lot of people get wrong.
So first of all Models have a vocabulary. Like if I am speaking English, I have a set of vocabulary, set of words which I can use to construct sentences.
Similarly, Qwen three has a vocabulary of around one fifty-one thousand nine thirty-six tokens. So let's assume one fifty K tokens.
When you ask a question, what is the capital of India? The answer is Delhi. But LLM does not just spit out Delhi.

It will spit out probability for all the tokens. All one fifty K tokens in its vocabulary. We are simply sampling, which means we are simply picking the one that we want, which is in this case would be the one with the highest probability.

So Mumbai, Bangalore, Washington could be lower probability, and Delhi has a probability of zero point seven one. So we have picked the top three, and out of top three we are displaying the top token, which is Delhi.
That's how models work and think, and that's why we need decoding strategies, which is mentioned in this very nice blog by Hugging Face.
Decoding strategies in large language models, around twenty twenty-four article. Okay, so we'll go back to our session.
To our lesson actually. Yep. So once we have understood the three intuitions, repeating them.
Model is a stack of repeated blocks. LLMs are auto regressive. They spit out one token at a time. And number three, the model outputs a probability distribution over the entire vocab.
Yeah. So the same thing is mentioned in this office hours as well. I think I've mo-I've moved to this office hours instead of the lesson. Let me go back to the lesson.
Lesson. Go to lesson one. Hide this transcript. Yeah, we're good to go.
Right. Now, we will think about which is our model that we want to target. Right? We will target Qwen three zero point six B. Why are we targeting such a small model?

Six hundred million parameter model. There are bigger models. You must be aware of frontier models. You must be using them daily, which is Claude Sonnet or Opus or GPT five point five or any other model that you're using.
Even open source models like Qwen three, Qwen three Coder, DeepSeek V3, V4, Min-Minimax, GLM.
I used an eight GB model quite a lot, which was called Microsoft... Microsoft model, which was called Phi-4 Multimodal instruct, right?
But we are not going to use that. We are going to study Qwen three point six B. Well, always we have this temptation that, we assume these models to be uninteresting toy projects, right?
But we must not forget that they are architecturally similar to these bigger models, and they offer us a good way to learn about LLM inferencing and training.
In fact, for most of the experiments, we'll be using the smaller GPT 2 model, right? So that GPT 2 model and Qwen three is what we'll be using.
Qwen three point six B has a newer cousin, which is called three point five zero point eight B. This is a multimodal model.

So these three are ones which we'll basically look at, right? Remember that this model is six hundred million parameter. Most of the models that you work with are at least two hundred times the size of this model or even more.
This Qwen three point five has had more than two point eight million downloads in a single month as per Hugging Face UI.
Right. Now let's move to Hugging Face. Hugging Face is the GitHub of models, and this is a good comparison, right? Now we will start thinking of models.
We saw the architecture of Qwen three point six B earlier over here, right? So we saw this. Now let's see how this model actually is— looks like on Hugging Face.
When the— When LLM teams upload these models onto Hugging Face or some other hub, what— how do they look like?
That's our main focus right now. And Hugging Face, as I said, is the GitHub for models, right?
If you understand, most of Hugging Fa— If you understand GitHub, you will sort of appreciate Hugging Face as well, right? It uses Git underneath.
So as you know, both GitHub and Hugging Face use Git under, Git underneath. Git is an open source tool, right?
So s-similar to GitHub, this Hugging Face also, sort of has commits, branches, pull requests, and issues.
Right. Now, the model card that you see, is ac— it has a README.md just like GitHub. MD stands for Markdown.
It is used extensively in GitHub. It is used extensively in Hugging Face and in Discord, right? So let's look at what is a Markdown file, and why is README.md so special, right?
So if you look at, any repo. So let's look at this repo. And if you create an issue A new issue.
So we are creating a new issue. Let's click on New Issue. Right. We can actually... So new issue, right?
And so let's put an heading here, that first issue for my code.
So when you preview this, you, you can instantly write code naturally using special symbols, and it will be rendered very beautifully.
So you can, you can write headings, you can write first subheading, right?
And it gets rendered very nicely. So you can do all the formatting. You can write whatever you want in plain text, it gets rendered nicely.

Lists can get rendered, task lists can get rendered. Code blocks can be created, right? And these code blocks are nice because instead of a messy sort of setup, you can... you have a copyable code block over here, right?
So to do a little bit more deep dive into this, we have got dillinger.io. This is a website where the author is basically showing us the full extent of Markdown.
So not only can you write, not only can you write headings, subheadings via text formatting, you can write lists, and lists are, lists are formatted in this manner.
As we showed, we can write task lists. You can write links and images. You can write block quotes. You can see block quotes look really nice.
In fact, let's try block quotes. It looks really pretty. This is the block quote we go to.
So we'll write it at the bottom. Kind of duplicate.
We'll remove this. Let's preview. Yeah, so you can see there's a nice, vertical line which separates.
Very, very pleasing, very good aesthetics, right? So you can write block quotes as well. So this is a good sort of a first look, and it can render math, equations very easily.
So you have got sort of MathJax-like, formatting which allows you to use. So let's try this as well Yeah, you can see it just rendered the inline math very well, very, very cleanly.

This is what we want. So Markdown very, very helpful, right? And what is the purpose of README.md? In GitHub, if you have a Markdown... If you have a README.md file, Markdown file, it's going to get rendered on UI immediately, on GitHub UI.
So on this route, I have a README.md, it's going to get rendered here. In lesson one, I have got two Markdown files. I can read this Markdown file as well by clicking on it, no problem.
But the README.md is going to get rendered by default and so you can keep on writing Markdown files. Very good for write.
Quarto uses Markdown file by default, so you can just write things regularly, format them and build really good blogs and content with ease.
So... And a side note on Markdown, which is given here. Markdown is the lingua franca or the language of choice of AI-assisted work.
and this is like a small unglamorous skill. I don't think it's unglamorous, but it, it has a big payback. it helps you a lot, right? So every model card is Markdown, every issue is Markdown.
Discord messages help are like in Markdown. You have Cursor Claude, which reads Markdown files very, very nicely, planning files and everything.

In fact, there is a post by Andrej Karpathy where he talks about building a personal LLM knowledge base, and he's basically suggesting that let AI tools sort of index and work out how your knowledge is—
how your knowledge exists on your laptop or on Obsidian or wherever. You have a list of— You have a bunch of Markdown files.
Let LLM build a personal knowledge base out of that. Let it connect things and understand what— how do you think about the world, you know.
So basically, it will be a personal LLM knowledge base, and this tweet went viral. So even there, Markdown was the cornerstone of his tweet, right?
We spoke about GitHub.md. sorry, README.md, right? So this post is, this part is covered. Now we will look at something... we'll go back to our... Yeah, we'll go back to this part.
So first is why does Hugging Face exist? and why not use GitHub, right? Because Hugging Face is trying to solve different problems.
There are engineering constraints. Like the biggest constraint is there with GitHub is that it won't allow a file which is more than hundred MB. There is a hard limit that GitHub puts on every file.
I think it's hundred MB, and it has not changed. You can commit those files, but when you try and push it to GitHub, it'll get rejected outrightly. GitHub has solved this problem.

It's not that this is— this problem has not been solved. So there is something called as Git LFS, which allows you to store large files, specifically blobs, large blobs, and LLM models are like large blobs, right?

So you can have a separate storage for them, and you only leave a small pointer to the repository. So you have normal files, your, your Python files, your React files, your TypeScript files, any other file that you have just gets uploaded,

gets committed, and pushed normally. There are pointers to these large files, and your single repo then works really well. So GitHub was using LFS— Hugging Face was using LFS, and they have transitioned to another package called Z in twenty twenty-five.
So let's look at any model, right? Now, to— Let's first figure out how to... Let's first figure out how to locate a model.
So one of the best ways is if you know the model, like I know Qwen three point six B, and it will display under models, and I can look at it.
The other way, if I do not know the model name, I can go through the organization and collections. So Qwen has published a lot of models, so it has around thirty-three collections.
We click on Collections, we go down. There is Qwen three point six, three point five, but we want Qwen three. Here is Qwen three. We click on it, and somewhere for...
Yeah. So this is Qwen three point six B. As I mentioned, README.md is always the model card is README.md, and it is shown here.
So the README.md file is sort of rendered or displayed here. Now we look at files and versions. We click on Files and Version, and these are the bunch of files that are there.

The license, there is README.md, there is config.json, then there is this model file, safetensors. I want you to look at wherever there is Z written, and specifically for large files, those are blobs.
So this is the actual Qwen3.6B file, model.safetensor. Remaining files have their own purposes, and they are needed to run this model, right?
This is what is mentioned here. We have got— If you open the repository of Qwen3.6B, you will— If you click on files and versions, you will see these bunch of files.
README.md, we have discussed. config.json is a critical file which we will discuss here. Apart from that, we will only discuss model.safetensor.
.safetensor.gguf.bin are the weights themselves. Right.
Let's look at config.json file. It is the blueprint, and then transformer is the builder. So you can think of it like that.
We will look at, architecture. We will look at hidden size. For now, we will look at vocab size. That's all that we care about, right?
Let me zoom. Yeah. So the same thing is visible on the model card. Not this, not the model card. Yeah. So let's click on config.json.
There is the architecture then, um, somewhere below, yeah. Right at the bottom, vocab_size is one five one nine three six.

and one example as well, and other classes are visible here as well. Right.

These are all fields from there. For three point five, you will see that the, that the classes Qwen three, five for conditional generation, right?
So wh-why does this class even exist? Because the weight is a bag of numbers, bag of tensors. And we, we want the— we want our— we want, we want these tensors to—

We, we have to multiply and these tensors have to work together, right? These— There is, there is multiplication, there are other operations involved, and then everything gets wired into a forward pass.
So in order for these operations, you need to create these classes and work with them.
So right now, Transformer library is doing the heavyweight with, with, with all these causal LM classes, and we will have to work and build our own Class in C to mirror this.

As I said, there'll be two worlds. One where transformer exists and when— one where transformer is not there, and we need to build our own class. So we have seen Hugging Face documentation page as well, right?

the answer is no. We will go the reverse way. We will first build, and we'll look at math, for example, in RoPE, in attention.

So with this, we'll be running that model file on your local, just like you run... just like you chat with ChatGPT or, with Cloud Chat, right?
You'll get similar prompt, and you can ask a question, maybe what is the capital of US or why is attention required?

how does it impact your output? We'll look at different flags within the model. We'll look at thinking on and off, right?

specifically columns like category and what is the length of certain fields, critical fields in that, right? So we want to understand the distribution, how, how big is the output.
